text.title: Feature Flag Rollout Runbook Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of feature-flag-rollout-runbook enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Rollouts proceed in stages 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%; each stage held minimum 1h or 1 traffic cycle (whichever longer).
rule.rationale: Tight first stages bound the blast radius if the change is bad; later stages confirm scale.

rule.statement: Cohort assignment uses a stable hash (user_id, session_id) — never random per request.
rule.rationale: Random-per-request cohorts toggle a user mid-flow and break funnels.

rule.statement: Auto-rollback fires on numeric SLO breach (error rate, latency, business metric) — not on subjective signals.
rule.rationale: Subjective rollbacks ('it looks weird') stall the rollout; numeric triggers act fast.

rule.statement: A kill switch (single-boolean override) exists separately from the rollout flag and can be flipped without redeploy.
rule.rationale: Composite-flag toggles take seconds an incident cannot afford.

rule.statement: Every flag has a cleanup due date ≤60 days post-100% — after which it is removed by an automated PR.
rule.rationale: Stale flags become hidden config that compounds tech debt and accident-risk.

rule.statement: If preconditions in `Applies If` are not all true, skip this methodology and route to the adjacent one referenced in Related.
rule.rationale: A skip rule keeps the decision tree closed: every branch ends at a real rule id.
